input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the serial robot MABI MAX 100, a six-axis machining robot equipped with a permanent magnet synchronous motor and gear transmission, designed for high-precision manufacturing operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides visualization services through a dashboard interface and optimization services by identifying joint friction to enhance the robot's motion accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for system structure and behavior description; architecture description languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector perspectives detailing components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition; MathWorks Simulink for simulation; and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java for detailed behavior implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system utilizes computational models structured across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical) using MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT). SysML IBDs and ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) define component & connector relationships, interfaces, and composition. Behavioral modeling is achieved through MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, while analytical simulations employ MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose code (C++, Java). Model integration across heterogeneous tools and stakeholder perspectives is required for comprehensive system description.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The system employs permanent magnet synchronous motors (input current I_M, output torque τ_M) coupled with cycloidal gears for actuation. Control is managed via Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl, enabling separate axis control with position, speed, or acceleration as command inputs.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Two position encoders per drive: a motor-mounted encoder and a secondary encoder behind the gearbox. Sensor data (input current and output angle) is streamed as time series via MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol for live-data linking between physical sensors and the digital model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Sensor data transmission uses MQTT protocol. Network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies, incorporating SysML [17] for modeling, architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25,26], and SysML IBDs [17,27] for component & connector-based architecture descriptions across requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical abstraction levels. Methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are applied for complexity management, with behavior modeling using MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines, integrated with tools like MathWorks Simulink [28], simulation methods [29,30], and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) to address heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives [31].

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100', its configuration ('six axes'), components ('permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission'), and its application ('high-precision manufacturing operations', 'machining'). The text explicitly states: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor... a gear transmission is first used', and 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'. The extraction is highly specific and well-grounded in the source.

The extraction correctly identifies visualization ('dashboard interface') and optimization ('identifying joint friction to enhance... motion accuracy') as services. The source supports this: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard' and 'resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction... to enhance the robot’s motion accuracy'. The extraction is accurate and specific to the described system.

The extraction accurately reflects the tools and technologies listed in Section II.A. It correctly identifies 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE)' using 'SysML,' lists specific 'architecture description languages (ADLs)' like 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc,' and notes the use of 'MathWorks Simulink' and 'C++ and Java' for simulation and detailed behavior. The description is highly specific and well-grounded in the text.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions 'live data' and 'real-time' representations but does not provide the specific technical details required by the characteristic, such as synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or explicit temporal granularity needs. Quotes: 'real-time, data-driven representations', 'live-data linking as well as visualization'.

The document focuses exclusively on a single digital shadow instance for one specific robot axis and does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between twins. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100', 'The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow'.

The document provides a detailed 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' and architecture for the shadow itself, but explicitly differentiates the Digital Shadow from the Digital Twin. It states that a Digital Twin provides active components not detailed in the shadow's scope, thus the overall Digital Twin system constellation is not described. Quotes: 'differentiate digital shadows from digital twins based on their communication flow', 'A Digital Twin (DT) can provide such active components'.

While the document mentions Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and MQTT for internal data linking, it does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards for cross-system communication, or dependencies on external systems. Quotes: 'link the existing measurement data in a digital shadow', 'streamed via MQTT'.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately synthesizes the modeling approaches described in Section II.A ('Modeling Software and Systems') and the specific implementations in Section IV ('Application of Digital Shadow Concepts'). It correctly lists the MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), architecture description languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs), behavioral tools (MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines, Simulink, C++, Java), and the need for integrating heterogeneous models and stakeholders. The specific use of SysML for structural modeling (Fig. 3) and Simulink for analytical friction simulations (Fig. 4) corroborates the extraction details.

Correctly identifies the permanent magnet synchronous motor with specific inputs and outputs (I_M, τ_M) and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller with its control capabilities (position, speed, acceleration inputs). However, it omits the intermediate 'gear transmission' mentioned in the text ('a gear transmission is first used... followed by a cycloidal gear'), missing a technical detail of the drive train.

Accurately details the sensing infrastructure, specifying 'Two position encoders per drive: a motor-mounted encoder and a secondary encoder behind the gearbox' and confirming that 'Sensor data (input current and output angle) is streamed as time series via MQTT protocol.' This matches the source text perfectly.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the documents discuss improving accuracy and comparing simulated vs. measured data to 'derive the friction' (Section IV.A), they do not explicitly define formal model fidelity requirements, specific V&V methods (beyond the general comparison), or a structured uncertainty quantification process. The text focuses on the implementation of the shadow and the identification of friction models rather than a formal fidelity and validity framework. Therefore, the absence of specific fidelity/validity details justifies the 'Not in Document' claim.
